title: Menemen
subtitle: Turkish scrambled eggs with tomato and pepper
excerpt: Menemen is the Turkish breakfast standard: eggs scrambled gently into a sauce of tomato, green pepper, and olive oil until just set and still slightly runny. The eggs are not stirred constantly but folded in slow, wide movements so they set in soft, custardy folds rather than small dry curds.
sourceNotes:
• Claudia Roden, *Arabesque* (2005). Principal reference for the Turkish regional version and the low-heat folding technique.
• Yotam Ottolenghi and Sami Tamimi, *Jerusalem* (2012). Cross-reference for the pepper and tomato ratio and the pul biber finishing note.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The tomato and pepper base can be made 30 minutes ahead and kept warm. Add the eggs just before serving.

glossaryTerms.definition: A Turkish dish of eggs scrambled into a tomato and pepper sauce, cooked low and slow until just set. Named after the town of Menemen near Izmir in western Turkey. Traditionally served in the pan it was cooked in, with bread for scooping.

content.text: is made in a single pan and takes about 15 minutes. The method matters: the tomato and pepper cook first until completely soft, then the eggs go in over low heat and are folded rather than stirred. If the heat is too high or the eggs are worked too actively, they turn granular and dry. The target is custardy folds that still wobble slightly when the pan is moved.

There is an ongoing argument in Turkey about whether onion belongs in menemen. This version includes it, briefly cooked, because it adds sweetness and body to the tomato base. Whether to add onion is a choice to make deliberately rather than by accident — it is that kind of dish. Serve with crusty bread or flatbread for scooping directly from the pan.

items.prepNote: crushed by hand in the tin

content.text: A wide frying pan or shallow sauté pan gives the best surface area for the eggs to set evenly. Traditionally menemen is cooked and served in a small copper pan.

Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide frying pan over a medium heat. Add {{pepper-green}} of diced green pepper (and {{onion}} of diced onion if using) and cook for 5 to 6 minutes, stirring occasionally, until completely soft. Add {{tinned-tomatoes}} of crushed tinned tomatoes, season with salt and pepper, and simmer for 6 to 8 minutes until the sauce has thickened and most of the liquid has cooked off. The base should be thick enough that a spoon drawn through it holds a path for a few seconds.

Reduce the heat to low. Pour {{eggs}} of lightly beaten eggs over the tomato sauce. Leave for 20 seconds until the edges begin to set, then fold gently with a spatula using slow, wide strokes. Continue folding every 15 to 20 seconds until the eggs are just set but still slightly runny in places. Remove from the heat — residual heat will finish the cooking.
Scatter {{aleppo-pepper}} of pul biber over the top and serve immediately, straight from the pan.

items.cause: The heat was too high or the eggs were stirred too quickly.
items.fix: Menemen needs a low heat and patient folding. If the eggs are already overcooked, they cannot be rescued — the base can still be used for the next batch.

items.symptom: Tomato sauce is too watery and eggs won't set
items.cause: The tomato base was not reduced enough before the eggs were added.
items.fix: Continue reducing the tomato base until it holds a path when a spoon is drawn through it. Adding eggs to a watery base dilutes them and makes even setting impossible.

items.cause: Pan temperature was still high from the tomato-cooking phase.
items.fix: After reducing the tomato sauce, wait 30 seconds before adding the eggs to let the pan temperature drop. Use the lowest possible heat setting.

content.text: Menemen is the default Turkish breakfast dish, eaten in homes, cafes, and lokanta restaurants from Istanbul to the Aegean coast. It is named after the town of Menemen near Izmir, where the combination of tomatoes, peppers, and eggs is thought to have been codified. The dish arrived in Britain through Turkish restaurants and the broader spread of Middle Eastern cooking, and is now made in home kitchens as a quick weekday breakfast. The pul biber finish is optional but traditional — the mild, fruity heat of Turkish chilli flakes lifts the egg and tomato without overpowering them.
